title: Classification of goods & money
summary:
## The big idea Not all goods behave the same way in a market. Economists classify goods using two simple properties — **excludability** and **rivalry** — plus the **value judgements** society attaches to them. This classification directly explains *why markets fail* and *where governments step in*, so it sets up much of the rest of the course. ## Two key properties - **Excludability** — can non-payers be *prevented* from consuming the good? - **Rivalry** — does one person's consumption *reduce* the amount available to others? | | **Rival** | **Non-rival** | |---|---|---| | **Excludable** | Private good (e.g. a sandwich) | Quasi-public / club good (e.g. pay-TV) | | **Non-excludable** | Common resource (e.g. ocean fish) | **Public good** (e.g. national defence) | ## Free vs economic goods A **free good** is not scarce and has no opportunity cost (e.g. air). Almost everything else is an **economic good** — scarce, with an opportunity cost. ## Private goods Most goods are **private goods**: they are **excludable** (non-payers can be shut out) and **rival** (if I eat the sandwich, you cannot). Markets can supply these efficiently because sellers can charge for them. ## Public goods and the free-rider problem **Public goods** are **non-excludable** and **non-rival** — think of national defence or street lighting. Because you *cannot* stop non-payers from benefiting, everyone has an incentive to let others pay and enjoy it for free: the **free-rider problem**. As a result the market provides **too little or none at all** — a form of **market failure** — so the government usually has to provide public goods, funded by taxation. **Quasi-public goods** have these features only *partly* — a road is non-rival until it gets congested; a beach can be partly excluded. They sit between private and pure public goods. ## Merit and demerit goods These rest on **value judgements** and **information failure**. A **merit good** (education, healthcare, vaccination) is **under-consumed** by the market because people undervalue its long-term private benefits and its positive spillovers to society — so governments encourage it. A **demerit good** (tobacco, alcohol) is **over-consumed** because people undervalue its harms and negative spillovers — so governments discourage it. Because they depend on value judgements, what counts as a merit or demerit good is **normative**. ## Money Exchange needs **money**. Without it, trade relies on **barter**, which needs a ‘double coincidence of wants’. Money performs **four functions**: a **medium of exchange**, a **measure/unit of value**, a **store of value** (undermined by inflation) and a **standard of deferred payment** (enabling credit). Good money is acceptable, durable, portable, divisible, uniform and limited in supply. ## Real-world application Governments everywhere provide public goods — from flood defences in the Netherlands to street lighting worldwide — precisely because markets will not. And most countries subsidise merit goods (free schooling) while taxing demerit goods (tobacco duties in the UK, sugar taxes in Mexico), showing the classification driving real policy.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Classify goods by **excludability** and **rivalry**. - **Private good** = excludable + rival → markets supply it. - **Public good** = non-excludable + non-rival → **free-rider problem** → market under-provides → government provision. - **Quasi-public good** = partly public (roads, beaches). - **Merit good** = under-consumed (education); **demerit good** = over-consumed (tobacco) — both rest on **value judgements**. - **Money**: replaces barter; 4 functions — medium of exchange, measure of value, store of value, standard of deferred payment.
revisionNotes:
# Classification of goods & money — quick revision

**Two properties**
- **Excludable?** can non-payers be shut out. **Rival?** does one person's use reduce what's left.

**Types**
- **Private** = excludable + rival → markets work.
- **Public** = non-excludable + non-rival → **free-rider problem** → under-provided → government provides.
- **Quasi-public** = partly public (roads, beaches).
- **Merit** = under-consumed (education, healthcare); **Demerit** = over-consumed (tobacco, alcohol) — both **normative** (value judgements).

**Free vs economic good**
- Free = not scarce, no opportunity cost; economic = scarce.

**Money**
- Replaces **barter** (no double coincidence of wants).
- 4 functions: medium of exchange, measure of value, store of value (hit by inflation), standard of deferred payment.
questions:
# Practice questions — Classification of goods & money **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A pure public good is best described as: - A — excludable and rival - B — non-excludable and non-rival - C — provided free by firms - D — a good with no demand
▸ Show answer

B. Public goods are non-excludable and non-rival (e.g. defence), which causes the free-rider problem.

**2.** The free-rider problem means that: - A — goods are always overproduced - B — people consume a good without paying, so markets under-provide it - C — firms make excessive profits - D — the government provides too many goods
▸ Show answer

B. Because non-payers cannot be excluded, they free-ride, so the market under-provides the good.

**3.** A merit good is one that is: - A — over-consumed because its harms are undervalued - B — under-consumed because its benefits are undervalued - C — non-rival and non-excludable - D — never provided by the government
▸ Show answer

B. Merit goods (e.g. education) are under-consumed as people undervalue their benefits/positive spillovers.

**4.** Which function of money is most weakened by high inflation? - A — medium of exchange - B — measure of value - C — store of value - D — standard of deferred payment
▸ Show answer

C. Inflation erodes purchasing power over time, undermining money's store-of-value function.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A town has no privately provided street lighting because firms cannot charge individual users, and residents admit they would rather let neighbours pay. The council also worries that too few people attend health check-ups. **(a)** Define ‘public good’. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, using the passage, why street lighting is not provided by the market. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse why health check-ups (a merit good) are under-consumed. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the government should provide both street lighting and health check-ups. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A good that is non-excludable and non-rival. [1+1]

(b) Non-excludable → cannot charge/exclude non-payers → free-rider problem → firms cannot profit → not provided. Use the data.

(c) Information failure + undervalued positive spillovers → private benefit seen as lower than social benefit → under-consumption.

(d) For: corrects market failure, positive externalities, equity. Against: cost/opportunity cost, government failure, over-provision. Judgement.

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with examples, the difference between a public good and a merit good. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether all merit goods should be provided free by the government. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Public = non-excludable + non-rival (technical); merit = under-consumed due to undervalued benefits (normative). Examples for each.

(b) For: raises consumption, equity, positive externalities. Against: cost, over-consumption when free, government failure, better to subsidise/inform. Judgement.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the four functions of money. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether money is more useful than barter in a modern economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Medium of exchange, measure of value, store of value, standard of deferred payment — define each with an example.

(b) Money removes the double coincidence of wants, enables specialisation, credit and saving; barter is slow/limited. Caveat: inflation harms the store-of-value function. Judgement clearly in favour of money.

keyTerms.definition: A good that is both excludable and rival, so markets can provide it.
A good that is non-excludable and non-rival, leading to the free-rider problem.
The inability to prevent non-payers from consuming a good.
When one person's consumption does not reduce the amount available to others.
When people consume a good without paying, so markets under-provide it.
A good with public-good features only in part (e.g. roads, beaches).
A good under-consumed by the market because its benefits are undervalued (e.g. education).
A good over-consumed by the market because its harms are undervalued (e.g. tobacco).
Direct exchange of goods without money, needing a double coincidence of wants.

tips: Define public goods with **both** properties (non-excludable AND non-rival) and link to the **free-rider problem**.
Stress that **merit/demerit goods rest on value judgements** (normative), unlike the technical public-good definition.
Give crisp examples: defence/street-lighting (public), education (merit), tobacco (demerit), roads (quasi-public).
Connect classifications forward to **government intervention** (provision, subsidies, taxes).
For money, learn the **four functions**; note **inflation** undermines the store-of-value function.
diagrams: No diagram needed here — but merit/demerit goods link to the **externality (MSB/MSC)** diagrams in the market-failure chapters; reference them for evaluation.
